coredata.dc:title: Electrochemical Oscillation in Li-Ion Batteries
coredata.dc:creator: Li, De
Sun, Yang
Yang, Zhenzhong
Gu, Lin
Chen, Yong
Zhou, Haoshen
coredata.dc:description: Two-phase reactions are prevalent in Li-ion batteries, whereas the underlying dynamics of phase separation in a real electrode still remain elusive, since numerous electrode particles constitute a formidably complex system for existing experimental techniques. Here we present an intriguing oscillatory phenomenon in the typical phase-separating electrode material Li4Ti5O12. During galvanostatic processes, the voltage oscillates due to the discrete nature of multi-particle phase-separating reactions, and the subtle oscillatory signals allow us to evaluate the fraction of actively phase-separating particles in real time. Through the analysis of oscillatory phenomena, we unveil the dependence of the active fraction on the depth of charge/discharge, cycling current, and working temperature, considerably deepening our understanding of the multi-particle phase-separation reaction. Moreover, it is the first time that electrochemical oscillations have been identified in rechargeable battery systems, opening up a new frontier for both theoretical and experimental researchers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: electrochemistry
lithium ion battery
spinel Li4Ti5O12
surface modification
phase transition
voltage oscillation
many-particle system
phase separation
discrete reaction
sequential dynamics

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge X.Z. Liu, J. Okagaki, Y.B. Zhu, A. Yamamoto, and Y.Q. Deng for experimental support. The work partially obtained support from the National Basic Research Program of China ( 2014CB932300 ) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21633003 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Anomalous Oscillations in the Expected Flat Voltage Plateau

(A–D) Galvanostatic charge/discharge of surface-modified (A) and commercial (B) Li4Ti5O12 electrodes (voltage versus Li metal; current rate: 0.1C, with 1C = 175 mA g−1). Magnified views in (C) and (D) clearly show the oscillatory voltage of the former one. Selected regions from I to VI in (C) are further magnified to give details of the voltage oscillations. The arrows in (C) and (D) represent the direction of charge and discharge, respectively.
Theoretical Galvanostatic Voltage Response of a One-/Several-Particle System with Non-monotone Chemical Potential

(A) Single-particle voltage response to the galvanostatic process (blue solid line), where no nucleation event is assumed. The hypothesized homogeneous (de)lithiation process is indicated by the red dashed line. Foe details see Section S2 of Supplemental Information.

(B) Schematic showing the inter-particle mosaic instability of a five-particle system, according to Burch and Dreyer et al. 14,15 Owing to the inhomogeneity, one particle will reach the critical Li concentration first and initiates phase separation, accompanied by a sudden rise in voltage. Consequently, the rapid Li insertion into this particle suppresses the lithiation of other particles, even leading to release of Li ions from those “inactive” particles, until the phase transition is completed in the “active” particle. This process will be repeated until all particles become fully lithiated. The corresponding voltage response of all single particles is shown below, with blue/red arrows indicating uptake/release of Li ions in each particle.
(C) Overall voltage response of the five-particle system in (B). The sudden rises in voltage are caused by discrete phase-separation events.

(A) Schematic illustration of the galvanostatic charge/discharge profile with voltage oscillation, where the occurrences of phase-separation events are denoted by green dots. In the normal voltage plateau region without oscillation, phase-separation events are uniformly random. For the oscillatory region, by contrast, there is a quasi-discrete distribution of phase-separation events.

(B) Schematic showing the phase transitions the occur in the oscillatory regime upon discharging. For each oscillatory period (from one valley to the next one), a group of particles starts phase separation from the nth valley. Before reaching the next (n + 1)th valley, almost no other phase-separation events occur due to the discrete distribution. Thus the capacity associated with the nth period is entirely provided by electrode particles activated around the nth valley. Under galvanostatic conditions, the fraction of phase-separating particles is proportional to the capacity of the current period.

(A) Galvanostatic charge/discharge profiles of the m-LTO electrode under varying applied currents at room temperature. The data are shifted along the vertical axis (0.05C: ±4 mV; 0.10C: ±8 mV; 0.20C: ±12 mV) for viewing convenience.
(B and C) Calculated active fractions and local current rates varying with the depth of charge (B) and discharge (C).

(A) Galvanostatic charge/discharge profiles of the m-LTO electrode under a current of 0.1C at different working temperatures. The data are shifted along the vertical axis (45°C: ±4 mV; 35°C: ±8 mV; 25°C: ±12 mV; 15°C: ±16 mV) for viewing convenience.
(B and C) Calculated active fractions and local current rates varying with the depth of charge (B) and discharge (C).

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing National Laboratory for Condensed Matter Physics, Institute of Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100190, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing National Laboratory for Condensed Matter Physics

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Two-phase reactions are prevalent in Li-ion batteries, whereas the underlying dynamics of phase separation in a real electrode still remain elusive, since numerous electrode particles constitute a formidably complex system for existing experimental techniques. Here we present an intriguing oscillatory phenomenon in the typical phase-separating electrode material Li4Ti5O12. During galvanostatic processes, the voltage oscillates due to the discrete nature of multi-particle phase-separating reactions, and the subtle oscillatory signals allow us to evaluate the fraction of actively phase-separating particles in real time. Through the analysis of oscillatory phenomena, we unveil the dependence of the active fraction on the depth of charge/discharge, cycling current, and working temperature, considerably deepening our understanding of the multi-particle phase-separation reaction. Moreover, it is the first time that electrochemical oscillations have been identified in rechargeable battery systems, opening up a new frontier for both theoretical and experimental researchers.

Despite remarkable achievements in developing Li-ion batteries over recent decades, many fundamental scientific issues underpinning Li-ion batteries are far from being adequately understood. Among them is the phase-separating reaction, which is prevalent in battery electrode materials, whereas collective phase behavior remains elusive due to the numerous nanoparticles in a real electrode. Here we report an electrochemical oscillation phenomenon originating from the discretely phase-separating behavior in a multi-particle electrode. The oscillation signals enable us to directly monitor the fraction of actively phase-separating electrode particles in real time, making a major advance in the fundamental understanding of phase-separating battery materials. This finding builds a bridge between the macroscopic electrochemistry and microscopic dynamics of the phase-separating reaction, filling a critical gap in our understanding of multi-particle storage systems with phase-separating materials.

Electrochemical oscillation phenomenon is observed in a rechargeable battery for the first time, suggesting a self-reorganization of the multi-particle phase-separation dynamics. The subtle oscillatory signals can serve as an indicator for the fraction of actively phase-separating particles in real time. Consequently, the dependence of active fraction on the depth of charge/discharge, applied current rate, and working temperature are unambiguously revealed. This finding builds a bridge between the macroscopic electrochemistry and the microscopic phase-separating reaction in multi-particle systems.

ce:section.ce:para: Ranging from portable electronics to electric vehicles, rechargeable Li-ion batteries (LIBs) have become an increasingly vital device for electrical energy storage in our daily life. 1,2 During charge/discharge, Li ions shuttle between the anodic and cathodic host materials. In general, the (de)intercalation of Li ions may proceed homogeneously (solid-solution behavior) or inhomogeneously (phase-separation behavior), and the latter is widespread in LIB electrode materials within certain compositional ranges. 3,4 Until now, however, the phase-separating reaction in a real electrode has remained poorly understood, as the battery electrode is a rather complex system comprising numerous particles (>1010) connected by binders and conductive additives, constituting a substantial challenge for both theoretical simulations and experimental observations. Olivine-type lithium iron phosphate (LiFePO4) was often used to study the electrochemically phase-separating behavior. 5–7 The global ratio of two phases (Li-rich and Li-poor phases) over the LiFePO4 electrode could be determined by diffraction techniques, 6,8–10 which however lacks spatial resolution and therefore neglects the particle-level phase distribution. This may explain why there was a fairly intuitive consensus that the phase separation concurrently occurred in almost all particles of the electrode.

Delmas et al. questioned the concurrently phase-separating scenario for the first time and proposed that almost all particles were either fully lithiated or fully delithiated during charge/discharge, 11 depicting a picture of sequential phase separation (termed as a “domino-cascade” model) that was verified by later studies. 12,13 Theoretical analysis also suggested that the sequential phase separation is thermodynamically more favorable in a multi-particle system. 14,15 Recently, Chueh et al. employed spatially resolved X-ray absorption spectroscopy to characterize hundreds of particles in a half-charged LiFePO4 electrode, 13 achieving the quantitative analysis of actively phase-separating particles in a phase-separation electrode for the first time. With the same technique, Li et al. found that the fraction of active particles increases with the cycling current. 16 More recently, Zhang et al. utilized microbeam X-ray diffraction (XRD) to monitor individual LiFePO4 nanoparticles under realistic operando conditions, 17 revealing more details of the multi-particle phase-separating behavior. These achievements are remarkable and informative for understanding the phase-separation reactions; nevertheless, spectroscopic measurements and analyses of hundreds to thousands of individual particles are costly and time-consuming. Therefore, the amount of work required to gain a full understanding of the phase separation in a real electrode could be prohibitive.

Lithium titanate (Li4Ti5O12) is another typical material that exhibits two-phase separation upon (de)lithiation, 18–20 with a three-dimensional spinel framework enabling efficient Li-ion transport and the “zero-strain” characteristic minimizing the energy penalty imposed by coherent phase boundaries. The Li4Ti5O12 electrode was able to deliver excellent battery performance even without conductive materials, 21 making it an ideal choice for studying the multi-particle phase-separating behavior. Employing the surface-treated Li4Ti5O12 electrode, we identified an oscillatory phenomenon within the phase-separation regime during charge/discharge, indicating a discrete nature of the multi-particle phase-separating reaction. The obtained subtle oscillation signals can directly reflect the fraction of actively phase-separating particles in real time. Statistical analysis suggests a strong dependence of the active fraction on the depth of charge/discharge, cycling current, and operating temperature, unveiling the dynamic behavior of multi-particle phase-separating reactions. More importantly, it is the first electrochemical oscillation ever found in a rechargeable battery system.

The surface-modified Li4Ti5O12 (m-LTO) contains ca. 49.5 wt% carbon black according to the thermogravimetric analysis (Figure S1). Compared with the commercial Li4Ti5O12 (c-LTO), the particles in the m-LTO sample grow up evidently due to the calcination process (Figure S2). Refined XRD patterns indicate the presence of ca. 20% rutile TiO2 in the m-LTO sample (Figure S3). The atomic structure of both samples was characterized using aberration-corrected scanning transmission electron microscopy, as shown in Figure S4. 22,23 For the c-LTO sample, the high-angle annular-dark-field (HAADF) images show a well-crystallized spinel structure at both the bulk and surface areas of the particle. In the case of m-LTO, significant reconstruction is observed on the particle surface, analogous to surface structures reported elsewhere. 24,25

Galvanostatic charge/discharge profiles of both c-LTO and m-LTO electrodes are shown in Figures 1A and 1B. At first glance, both electrodes deliver fairly flat voltage plateaus, a characteristic feature of the two-phase reaction. From magnified views (Figures 1C and 1D), however, we can see that the voltage profile of m-LTO electrode begins to oscillate in the plateau region, with decreasing oscillatory periods upon charge/discharge. After being triggered, the amplitude of voltage oscillation quickly increases to the order of 1 mV (regions I and IV in Figure 1C), then holds steady around this level (regions II and V), and finally fades away at the end of the voltage plateau (regions III and VI). Since the m-LTO electrode contains ca. 20% rutile TiO2, which is also active for lithium storage, 26,27 we evaluated the influence of TiO2 using a mixed c-LTO and rutile TiO2 (4:1 in weight) electrode (Figure S5). The resulting charge/discharge profiles exhibit a flat plateau with no oscillation, suggesting that the voltage oscillation of m-LTO electrode cannot be attributed to the TiO2 impurity. The unexpected oscillatory phenomenon within the two-phase regime necessitates reconsideration of the phase-separation scenario in a multi-particle electrode.

The voltage response of a single particle with non-monotone chemical potential has been well documented in previous studies. 7,15,28 In brief, the galvanostatic voltage would experience a bump overshooting the two-phase equilibrium voltage when entering the phase-separating regime (Figure 2 A). For a several-particle system, Burch proposed that once a certain particle initiates phase separation, the Li insertion into other particles would be suppressed due to the lower chemical potential of the actively phase-separating particle, until the phase transition is completed in this “active” particle (Figure 2B). 14 This scenario is referred to as “mosaic instability.” Consequently, the phase separation follows a one-by-one pathway in the several-particle system. Later, Dreyer and coworkers interpreted the inherent voltage hysteresis of a many-particle phase-separation electrode by accounting for the sequentially particle-by-particle phase separation. 15 Furthermore, a general model was constructed to study the phase behavior of many-particle storage systems. 29,30 Considering the effect of mosaic instabilities, Bazant's group simulated the voltage response of nanoparticulate phase-separating electrodes through numerical modeling. 31 For the case of a few particles, the galvanostatic voltage profile exhibits many sudden rises and drops due to the sequential mosaic instability events (Figure 2C), 15,31–34 reflecting the discrete nature of phase separation in a many-particle system. But when it comes to a real electrode, the mosaic instability is actually a local phenomenon since it requires sufficiently rapid inter-particle exchanges of both Li ions and electrons. 31,33 The randomly occurring mosaic instability events in many quasi-isolated local domains would be smoothed out when averaged over the electrode, and therefore a flat voltage plateau is usually observed in the two-phase regime of multi-particle phase-separation electrodes.

Voltage oscillations observed in this work resemble the theoretical prediction for a several-particle system, suggesting a discrete distribution of mosaic instability events rather than the uniform one in non-oscillatory regimes (Figure 3 A and Section S2 in Supplemental Information). Essentially it is an orderly behavior of the collective phase-separation dynamics. There exists a thermodynamic driving force for voltage oscillations, as evident in the lower/higher charge/discharge voltage compared with the values extrapolated from the non-oscillatory regimes (Section S3 in Supplemental Information). Intriguingly, the extrapolated voltage profiles almost connect all peaks/valleys in the oscillatory regime during charge/discharge, closely resembling the theoretical analysis on the inherent voltage hysteresis of multi-particle phase-separation systems in Dreyer et al. 15 In other words, the oscillatory phenomenon tends to diminish the inherent voltage hysteresis caused by the multi-particle effect, and therefore we may reasonably say that the discretely “group-by-group” 31 phase separation (oscillatory regimes) is energetically more favorable than the uniformly random one (non-oscillatory regimes).

Within one oscillation period, i.e., from one peak/valley to the next during charge/discharge, the capacity is almost exclusively contributed by the particles that initiate phase separation at the beginning of the period (Figure 3B). Assuming a mean particle size, the oscillating period (in capacity) is proportional to the number of active particles. Accordingly, both the active fraction and local current on active particles can be obtained by analyzing the subtle oscillatory signals (for details see Section S4 in Supplemental Information). The decreasing oscillation period in Figure 1 indicates that the number of actively phase-separating particles decreases with the depth of charge/discharge. The downward trend of active fractions, albeit derived from the oscillatory signals, could also be extrapolated to the non-oscillatory regime. This trend probably explains why the voltage oscillation appears in the late stage of the two-phase regime. Before the oscillation emerges, the random phase-separation events are accompanied by statistical fluctuations, which are inversely proportional to the square root of the number of random processes (Section S5 in Supplemental Information). Since the number of actively phase-separating particles decreases with the depth of charge/discharge, the relative magnitude of fluctuations continues to increase until reaching a critical value that induces a quasi-discrete distribution of phase-separation events, which manifests as oscillations in voltage. The aforementioned assumptions could be reflected in regions I and IV of Figure 1C, wherein the voltage oscillations were launched almost instantaneously rather than accumulated gradually.

Oscillatory phenomena of the m-LTO electrode at various cycling currents are displayed in Figure 4 A. The analyzed active fractions and local currents are shown in Figures 4B and 4C. In all currents examined, the active fraction decreases with the depth of charge/discharge. For a smaller applied current, the active fraction is lower during the entire charge/discharge process, namely, a smaller number of active particles at any given time. As already mentioned, a smaller number of active particles corresponds to a larger magnitude of fluctuations, which may explain why the oscillatory phenomena appear earlier at lower current rates. Conversely, high working currents would reduce the relative magnitude of fluctuations and thus delay or even prevent voltage oscillations, as verified by the diminishing oscillation when applying higher currents (Section S6 in Supplemental Information). In contrast, phase-field modeling results predicted that oscillations begin earlier for the larger currents in a porous LiFePO4 electrode. 32 The opposite trend in current dependency is probably due to fact that the internal phase separation and nucleation in LiFePO4 nanoparticles, proposed theoretically by Cogswell et al. 35,36 and later experimentally confirmed by Lim et al., 37 were neglected in the early models.

Under even higher currents, as shown in a very recent paper investigating phase behaviors of the Li4Ti5O12 electrode through phase-field modeling, 38 the charge/discharge progress will adopt a concurrent delithiation/lithiation mechanism, which fundamentally eliminates the voltage oscillations. Moreover, the phase separation itself would be suppressed by high applied currents, which has been theoretically predicted 7,39 and then experimentally confirmed. 40–42 In the case of LiFePO4 electrode, for instance, it was demonstrated that phase separation in the LiFePO4 nanoparticles would be suppressed at high currents due to the limitation of reaction kinetics on the phase boundary, thus adopting a non-equilibrium reaction pathway. 7,43

The influence of global currents on local currents suggests a significant asymmetry between charge and discharge: no apparent change in local currents is observed under varying charge currents; by contrast, the local current rises significantly when applying a higher discharge current. That is, increases in both active fraction and local current jointly contribute to the increasing discharge current; by contrast, the growing active fraction is almost solely responsible for the increasing charge current. The increase in active fractions at higher applied currents is consistent with a recent report concerning olivine LiFePO4, 16 where the multi-particle electrode mainly accommodates the increasing current by raising the active fraction of electrode particles. A notable charge-discharge asymmetry was identified in both experimental observations and theoretical simulations, 16 i.e., the active fraction is more current dependent during discharge with respect to that during charge, qualitatively consistent with the current dependence of the m-LTO electrode. Employing the synchrotron microbeam diffraction technique, Zhang et al. 17 suggested that the increase in the active fraction, together with the increase in the local current, contributes to the higher applied current of nanoparticulate LiFePO4 electrode, analogous to the discharge process of the m-LTO electrode in this work. The asymmetry between charge and discharge was further studied by Lim et al., 37 who examined by operando X-ray microscopy the local exchange current and phase distribution. Results showed that the phase separation was suppressed during discharge but enhanced during charge, partly accounting for the rate-dependent asymmetry in active fraction. 16 Despite being associated with the physical properties of the olivine LiFePO4 structure (e.g., the anisotropic Li+ transport and the consequent anisotropic phase morphologies), the above mechanistic studies are very enlightening for us in understanding the charge-discharge asymmetry of the m-LTO electrode. It is probable that the autocatalytic reactions (higher applied current rates enhance compositional non-uniformities) during charge and autoinhibitory reactions (higher applied current rates suppress compositional non-uniformities) during discharge are also applicable to the LTO particles. 44

Figure 5 A shows the galvanostatic charge/discharge profile of the m-LTO electrode at working temperatures from 15°C to 55°C. For all temperatures, the active fraction decreases with the depth of charge/discharge (Figures 5B and 5C). Moreover, the active fractions are considerably lower at elevated temperatures, explaining the earlier onsets of voltage oscillations. As the current rate is set to 0.1C, the lower active fractions indicate higher local currents on active particles. Intuitively, a higher working temperature may facilitate the ionic/electronic transport and thereby enable a higher fraction of active particles. Here the seemingly counterintuitive results, however, suggest that the elevated temperature reduces the number of active particles required to maintain the galvanostatic process by promoting the rate capability of single particles. This finding reminds us of the numerical simulation of a finite-particle phase-separating system performed by Dargaville and Farrell, 33 who demonstrated that increasing the value of exchange current density would decrease the resulting number of active particles.

The voltage oscillation phenomenon observed in the m-LTO electrode, for the first time, provides direct electrochemical evidence for the sequential scenario of multi-particle phase-separation reactions. Our findings confirm the theoretical prediction based on phase-field simulations: the discrete filling of storage particles will produce voltage oscillations in the charge/discharge curve during galvanostatic tests. 31–33,45 Within the oscillatory regime, the phase transition of numerous particles exhibits a discretely “group-by-group” behavior, i.e., the onsets of phase separation concentrate around each peak/valley of voltage oscillations during charge/discharge. According to our estimates, each group includes ca. 107–109 particles in the studied electrode, indicating that the collective phase behavior of numerous particles is highly ordered. From the thermodynamic point of view, the discrete phase separation is more energetically favorable with respect to the uniformly random picture, rationalizing the oscillatory phenomena. But meanwhile it raises a question: why was the beneficial oscillatory phenomenon rarely observed in previous studies of phase-separating battery electrodes? One possible reason is that the voltage bump of single composing particles (during the galvanostatic process), a key premise for the oscillatory phenomenon, could be diminished by nucleation events (see Figure S7B), which could be promoted by the surface wetting (Li+ accumulation at the particle surface) effect. 7,45 That may rationalize why the voltage oscillation cannot be observed in the c-LTO electrode—the perfectly crystallized surface of c-LTO nanoparticles benefits the Li-ion accumulation and the consequent surface nucleation will trigger phase separations ahead of the phase-separation regime in Figure 2A. In contrast, the passivated surface layer of m-LTO particles minimizes the surface wetting effect and thereby retains the voltage bump of the theoretical galvanostatic voltage response. Another key prerequisite for the voltage oscillation is the facile redistribution of both Li ions and electrons among electrode particles, which necessitates an efficient inter-particle network for the Li-ion/electron transport.

Appropriate test conditions are also necessary for observing the oscillatory phenomenon successfully. The well-established theory about many-particle storage systems by Dreyer et al. indicated that applying a small current is necessary to ensure the sequential phase-separation behavior, 29–31,34 which is essential for generating electrochemical oscillations. Yet simultaneously, a low applied current may reduce the oscillation amplitude (see Figure S17) and therefore adds to the difficulty of identifying the subtle oscillations in voltage. As shown in Figure S18, the oscillation peak can also be weakened at high working temperatures, especially during the charge process. A simulation study by Herrmann et al. demonstrated that the oscillatory peaks widen at a lower relaxation time of single particle and a higher entropic effect, 46 both of which correspond to elevated temperatures. Therefore, lowering the temperature is beneficial for detection of electrochemical oscillations, but at the same time a too low temperature will hinder the Li-ion transport, even leading to electrolyte solidification. Once the conditions are met, the oscillatory phenomenon could occur independent of the charge/discharge history, as reflected in the interrupt-rest-resume operation (Section S8 in Supplemental Information): after resuming the galvanostatic process from open circuit, both the amplitudes and periods of voltage oscillations quickly return to the pre-interruption level; furthermore, when the applied current is suddenly changed during galvanostatic processes, the oscillatory properties would be changed instantaneously. Therefore, we may reasonably say that the voltage oscillation is state dependent rather than path dependent.

The subtle oscillatory signals can serve as a real-time indicator for the fraction of actively phase-separating particles in multi-particle electrodes. From the analysis above, we have drawn several preliminary conclusions concerning the phase-separating dynamics. (1) Even with a very low global current, the local current on active particles could be rather high, consistent with previous studies of the LiFePO4 electrode. 16,17 As can be seen in Figure 5, a global current of 0.1C rate would generate local current rates of 5–80C varying with the temperature. The phase-field modeling of Li4Ti5O12 electrodes also suggested a very high current density on the actively discharging particles despite the low current rates, 38 in agreement with our results and analysis. (2) The active fraction decreases with the depth of charge/discharge, analogous to the numerical simulation results on a 26-particle phase-separating system, 34 causing a gradual increase in local current on actively phase-separating particles. This seems to be a natural result of the decrease in the number of particles available for further charge/discharge. If we focus on the particles that remain unreacted, the active fraction actually increases with the depth of charge/discharge (Section S9 in Supplemental Information). (3) Effects of applied currents on the phase-separation dynamics exhibit significant asymmetry between charge and discharge. Upon charging, the local current is almost invariant with the applied current, and the change in global current is mainly accommodated by adjusting the fraction of active particles accordingly. In the case of discharge, increasing the applied current raises both the active fraction and the local current. (4) The active fraction decreases with the increasing temperature, probably due to the promoted current-carrying capability of single particles at elevated temperatures. It is likely that the multi-particle phase-separation electrode follows the principle of “minimalism” during galvanostatic processes—that is, employing as few active particles as possible to maintain the global current. A probable explanation is that the electrochemically driven phase separation is energy consuming and thus tends to be minimized in scale. These findings deepen our understanding of the multi-particle phase-separating dynamics and thereby will contribute to more accurate mathematical simulations at electrode level. It is also worth noting that although the voltage oscillation is an attractive feature, its magnitude is comparable with that of the recently reported memory effect of the LiFePO4 electrode. 47 Therefore, the oscillatory phenomena have important technological implications and should be considered in battery management systems when the applied current is low and/or the battery is operated at a low temperature.

Besides the regular oscillatory phenomenon presented in this work, random fluctuations in the galvanostatic charge/discharge curve of LiFePO4 electrode were presented in Dreyer et al. 15 and then mathematically simulated by Ferguson and Bazant, 34 wherein the effect of size-dependent nucleation was demonstrated. Given the considerable difference in structural properties, the discrepancy in oscillatory behaviors between LiFePO4 and Li4Ti5O12 electrodes is not unexpected. Compared with the “zero-strain” Li4Ti5O12 with three-dimensional channels for Li-ion transport, the electrochemical phase behavior of LiFePO4 electrode would be greatly affected by the large coherency strain between the two end-member phases and anisotropic surface originating from a one-dimensional ionic pathway. 7,16,34–37,43 Very recently, Smith and Bazant predicted the existence of growing oscillations at the end of charge/discharge plateaus in graphite/LiFePO4 cells by employing the well-developed multi-phase porous electrode theory. 48 These achievements suggest that the electrochemical oscillation could be extended to other nanoparticulate electrodes involving two or more coexisting phases, which represent a broad spectrum of electrode materials for rechargeable Li-/Na-/Mg-/Al-ion batteries, and thus merits greater research effort toward a thorough understanding of the oscillation mechanism.

The oscillatory behavior in (electro)chemical systems was reported early in the 19th century, 49–51 and has undergone enormous investigations since the late 20th century. 52,53 Oscillatory phenomena merit in-depth research not only because of the cognitive interest but also because it can be very enlightening for us to understand the complex electrochemical/chemical processes. 52,54,55 It was generally recognized that the oscillatory phenomenon is closely associated with non-equilibrium thermodynamics. For instance, the periodic formation and dissolution of passivating surface layers would cause oscillations in electrocatalytic systems. 56,57 By contrast, the aforementioned electrochemical oscillation of the m-LTO electrode is essentially a self-reorganization of multi-particle phase-separating dynamics, representing a reaction pathway that is closer to the thermodynamic equilibrium. Although we here focus mainly on the galvanostatic voltage oscillation, the potentiostatic current oscillation, as another side of the same coin, has also been confirmed (Section S10 in Supplemental Information). The newly established electrochemical oscillation makes the first record in rechargeable battery systems and, moreover, broadens the categories of oscillatory phenomena and potentially will inspire more related research.

In this paper, we unambiguously show that the multi-particle phase-separation reaction may proceed discretely and that such a collective phase behavior manifests as oscillations in voltage during galvanostatic charge/discharge processes. This is the first time that the electrochemical oscillation has been identified in a rechargeable battery system. From the subtle oscillating signals, we can directly evaluate the fraction of actively phase-separating particles and thereby realize the real-time analysis of phase-separating dynamics in multi-particle electrodes. Our discovery and analysis promote the fundamental understanding of electrochemical processes in practical phase-separating electrodes, which is vital for the development of advanced battery materials. Furthermore, these findings could be extended to other phase-separating systems even beyond the field of LIBs, opening up a new dimension for further experimental and theoretical studies.

Commercial Li4Ti5O12 (c-LTO) was provided by Ishihara Sangyo Kaisha. To prepare the surface-modified Li4Ti5O12 (m-LTO), we mixed the c-LTO and acetylene black (1:1 in weight) and thoroughly ground the mixture in a mortar. The mixture loaded in a ceramic boat was transferred into a ceramic furnace. Under Ar atmosphere with a trace amount of air, the mixture was calcined at 800°C for 24 hr to obtain the product m-LTO.

The thermogravimetric measurement was performed on a TG-DTA instrument (Bruker TG-DTA2010SA), in which the sample was heated from room temperature to 1,000°C at a rate of 5°C min−1 with an air flow of 70 mL min−1. The morphologies and crystal structures were characterized by a scanning electron microscope (LEO Gemini Supra 35) and powder XRD (Cu-Kα radiation, Bruker D8 Avance Diffractometer). HAADF)imaging was performed using a JEOL 2100F (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) scanning transmission electron microscope operated at 200 kV with a CEOS (Heidelberg, Germany) probe aberration corrector.

In the coin-type battery (CR2032), a composite paste was pressed with a finger on carbon paper (GDL 35BA from SGL Group) to make the working electrode, where the composite paste contained 42.5 wt% active material, 42.5 wt% acetylene black, and 15.0 wt% polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE). Note that sample m-LTO includes both active material and acetylene black (ca. 50 wt%). The counter electrode of lithium metal was separated from the working electrode by a Celgard 2400 porous polypropylene film. After drying all components, the cells were assembled in a glovebox filled with Ar gas. The electrolyte was 1M lithium bis(trifluoromethanesulfonyl)imide (LiTFSI) dissolved in a mixture of carbonate-based solvents (ethylene carbonate and dimethyl carbonate, 1:1 in volume). Galvanostatic measurements were carried out using a Hokuto Denko HJ1001SD8 battery tester (Tokyo, Japan).

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge X.Z. Liu, J. Okagaki, Y.B. Zhu, A. Yamamoto, and Y.Q. Deng for experimental support. The work partially obtained support from the National Basic Research Program of China (2014CB932300) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21633003).

ce:section.ce:para: H.Z. directed the project. D.L. performed material synthesis, electrochemical tests, and data statistics. Z.Y. and L.G. performed the scanning transmission electron microscopy measurement. Y.S. interpreted the experimental data and proposed the mechanism. Y.S. wrote the paper. D.L., Y.S., Z.Y., L.G., Y.C., and H.Z. all contributed to the discussion.
The authors declare no competing interests.
Supplemental Information includes Supplemental Experimental Procedures and 26 figures and can be found with this article online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.joule.2018.03.014.

ce:e-component.ce:label: Document S1. Supplemental Experimental Procedures and Figures S1–S26
Document S2. Article plus Supplemental Information
